claim: The text-only section (entropy 3.9016 bits, 7 folios) represents unenciphered or minimally enciphered running text in medieval Latin or Northern Italian, and its word-frequency distribution should match a Zipf exponent significantly closer to 1.0 than the manuscript-wide exponent of 0.8946
evidence_for: Text-only section has the highest entropy of all sections (3.9016 bits), closest to natural Latin/Italian (~4.0/~3.95)
High-confidence belief (0.677) that text-only section approximates natural language most closely
7 folios is a small but statistically tractable sub-corpus for frequency analysis
Absence of illustrations in text-only folios removes the label/caption effect that depresses entropy elsewhere
evidence_against: Small sample (2,349 words) limits statistical power for reliable Zipf fitting
Elevated entropy could reflect a different cipher mode rather than absence of cipher
test: Compute Zipf exponent and R-squared for the text-only section in isolation; compute KL-divergence between text-only unigram distribution and a reference medieval Latin corpus unigram distribution
test_metric: Text-only Zipf exponent; KL-divergence against Latin reference
test_threshold: Confirmation if text-only Zipf exponent â‰¥ 0.95 and KL-divergence < 0.15; denial if Zipf exponent < 0.85 or KL-divergence > 0.30

tests_run.test: compute zipf exponent and r-squared for the text-only section in isolation; compute kl-divergence be
tests_run.details: Observed Zipf exponent: 0.8946 (R²=0.9084). Natural language expected: ~1.0. Fit quality: moderate.

reasoning: If the text-only section statistically resembles natural Latin more than any cipher section, it becomes the highest-priority target for direct frequency-matching decipherment attempts
